topic: Study of Religion: Christianity / Islam / Buddhism / Hinduism / Judaism / Sikhism
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Are all religions basically the same underneath?
items.correct: No - there are real overlaps, such as compassion and charity, but core claims differ sharply: theism versus non-theism, resurrection versus rebirth; precise study requires the specific tradition's own terms.
items.misconception: All six religions teach the same things with different names.
items.random_wrong: The six religions share a single founder.
items.why: Vague universalism produces inaccurate answers; examiners reward precise use of each tradition's distinct concepts.

items.q: Do all believers read their scriptures in the same way?
items.correct: No - every tradition contains literal, contextual and allegorical approaches, and internal diversity between denominations and schools is itself part of what you study.
items.misconception: Each religion has one official way of reading its scripture that all members accept.
items.random_wrong: Scriptures are only read by clergy, never by ordinary believers.
items.why: Treating traditions as monolithic flattens the internal debates that A-level questions frequently target.

items.q: Is religious authority only a matter of holy books?
items.correct: No - authority also flows through community tradition, leaders and teachers, conscience, reason and religious experience, and traditions weigh these differently.
items.misconception: Scripture is the only source of authority in any religion.
items.random_wrong: Religious authority is decided by national governments.
items.why: The specification's phrase is 'sources of wisdom and authority' in the plural; one-source answers miss the interplay.

items.q: What does the Study of Religion component require of your chosen religion?
items.correct: Examining its sources of authority, view of ultimate reality, beliefs about death, moral principles and expressions of identity
items.random_wrong: Covering all six religions in equal depth in one exam
items.why: Correct answer: Examining its sources of authority, view of ultimate reality, beliefs about death, moral principles and expressions of identity. “Studying only its festivals” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which pairing of religion and scripture is correct?
items.why: It's Sikhism - the Guru Granth Sahib. “Islam - the Vedas” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which term describes the oneness of God in Islam?
items.why: Correct answer: Tawhid. “Trinity” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Beliefs about 'self, death and afterlife' in Hinduism centre on what?
items.correct: Reincarnation of the atman according to karma until liberation (moksha)
items.misconception: Bodily resurrection on a final day of judgement
items.random_wrong: The complete end of existence at death with no continuity
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reincarnation of the atman according to karma until liberation (moksha). “Bodily resurrection on a final day of judgement” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
